pattern_recognition: Dynamic Programming; this problem can be categorized under dynamic programming as it involves breaking down the problem into smaller subproblems and using their solutions to build up to the final solution. The signals include the need to find the minimum number of operations and overlapping subproblems.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Generate all possible sequences of operations to convert word1 to word2 and find the one with the minimum operations.
approaches.algorithm: Recursively explore all possible operations (insert, delete, replace) for each character in both words.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use dynamic programming to build a table that stores the minimum edit distance for substrings of word1 and word2, allowing for efficient computation.
approaches.algorithm: Create a 2D DP array where dp[i][j] represents the minimum edit distance between the first i characters of word1 and the first j characters of word2. Fill the table based on previous computations.

dry_run: Consider word1 = 'horse' and word2 = 'ros'. Start with an empty DP table. Initialize the first row and column based on the length of the words. Fill in the table by comparing characters and applying the minimum operations needed. The final cell will give the minimum edit distance.
edge_cases: Both words are empty; one word is empty while the other is not; words are of length 1; words are identical; words have all characters the same except one; very large inputs that may cause performance issues.
common_pitfalls: 1. Forgetting to initialize the DP table correctly can lead to incorrect results. 2. Not considering all three operations (insert, delete, replace) can result in an underestimation of the edit distance. 3. Mismanaging the indices while filling the DP table can cause out-of-bounds errors or incorrect calculations.
